contents: Management of digital mucous cysts: a systematic reviewand treatment algorithmSamer Jabbour1, MD, Elio Kechichian2, MD, Roger Haber2, MD, Roland Tomb2, PHD, andMarwan Nasr1, MD1Department of Plastic and ReconstructiveSurgery, Faculty of Medicine, Saint JosephUniversity, Hotel Dieu de France Hospital,Beirut, Lebanon, and 2Department ofDermatology, Faculty of Medicine, SaintJoseph University, Hotel Dieu de FranceHospital, Beirut, LebanonCorrespondenceSamer Jabbour, MDDepartment of Plastic and ReconstructiveSurgeryFaculty of MedicineSaint Joseph UniversityHotel Dieu de France HospitalAlfred Naccache Blvd, AchrafiehBeirut 1100 2160LebanonE-mail: The first two authors contributed equally tothis review and are coauthors.Funding: None.Conflicts of interest: None.AbstractDigital mucous cysts (DMC) are benign, highly recurrent lesions of the digits. To date,there is still no treatment agreement on the treatment of DMC. Herein, we review availabledata on treatment modalities, including both surgical and nonsurgical techniques, and toprovide a practical algorithm for the management of DMC. A systematic review wasconducted using MEDLINE, EMBASE, and Cochrane databases. Articles studying themanagement of DMC were included in this review. A total of 40 articles were included inthe review. The five most frequently used treatments for DMC were surgery (n = 849),expression of cyst content (n = 132), sclerotherapy (n = 119), corticosteroid injection(n = 108), and cryotherapy (n = 103). Surgery yielded the highest cure rate among all

treatment modalities (95%) compared to sclerotherapy (77%), cryotherapy (72%),corticosteroid injection (61%), and expression of cyst content (39%) (P < 0.001). Surgeryshould be considered as the first-line treatment for DMC. Second-line treatments includesclerotherapy and cryotherapy. Third-line treatments include corticosteroid injections,expression of cyst content, and less-studied modalities. Surgery showed the highest curerates. Future adequately designed randomized controlled trials are warranted to comparedifferent treatment modalities.IntroductionDigital mucous cysts (DMC), also known as myxoid cysts, syn-ovial cysts, mucous cysts, and myxomatous cutaneous cysts,were first described in 1883 by Hyde as synovial lesions of theskin.1 These benign myxoid cysts typically involve the lateral ordorsal surfaces of the distal interphalangeal joint or the proximalnail fold on the fingers and less commonly on the toes2 and pre-sent as a 3-10 mm translucent, solitary smooth nodule.1,3,4Subungual mucous cysts and multiple mucous cysts are rareoccurrences.5 Mucous cysts tend to affect individuals between50 and 70 years old,6 women more than men (around 47% of

patients being males).7-9 The exact mechanism of DMC forma-tion is still unknown. The DMCs probably result from degenera-tive changes in the fibrous capsule of the joint or synovialtissue,10 especially that a communication between the cyst andadjacent joint space has been demonstrated.1 The developmentof osteophytes and an increase in synovial fluid associated withosteoarthritis (OA) are contributing factors to the formation ofDMCs since the prevalence of mucous cysts in OA ranges from6411 to 93%.7 Mucous cysts are considered pseudocysts sincethey do not contain a true epithelial lining and cyst wall. Theyare formed by a herniation of the joint capsule, and the lining ofthe DMC is in fact formed by fibroblasts surrounded by com-pressed connective tissue.4,12,13 The content of DMC is debat-able. Histology usually reveals a pseudocyst cavity within afibrous capsule containing a myxomatous stroma with scatteredfibroblasts.14 DMCs are often asymptomatic but may occasion-ally be associated with decreased range of motion, pain, tender-ness, nail deformities, and periodic spontaneous discharge.15Despite few reports of spontaneous regression, treatment isusually needed.8 Nonsurgical management such as puncturing,sclerotherapy, steroid injection, hyaluronidase injection, cryosur-gery,andcarbon-dioxidevaporizationhavebeenconsid-ered.12,16-19 Surgical treatment has also been reported withvarying cure rates and includes excision of the cyst, with orwithout osteophyte, followed by overlying skin removal and skingraft or flap coverage.7,10,20-23 However, to date, there is still noConstantet al. The purpose of thisarticle is to review all available data on available treatment tech-niques, surgical and nonsurgical, and to provide a practicalalgorithm for the management of DMC.Materials and methodsSearch descriptionOn March 29, 2016, we conducted a systematic review ofpublished articles in MEDLINE, EMBASE, and Cochranedatabases. The search was done with no restrictions usingspecific terms for DMC ("ganglionic cyst" or "ganglion cyst" or"myxoid cyst" or "mucous cyst" or "synovial cyst") combinedwith keywords to narrow the search to cysts located onfingers or toes ("digit" or "digits" or "digital" or "thumb" or"index" or "finger" or "toe" or "toes"). All studies related to thetreatment of DMC published in English or French until thesearch date were included in the systematic review. Only thestudies about DMC treatment were selected. Cure wasdefined as complete healing of the cyst with no recurrenceduring the reported follow-up period. Patients lost to follow-upwere excluded from this study. All included articles clearlydescribe the treatment modality and the number of curedlesions. Exclusion criteria were (i) case-series withfive lesions or less and (ii) studies using concomitanttreatments.The five most commonly used treatments were chosen formodalities were compared using a chi-square test. Asignificance level of 0.05 was chosen. Statistical analysis wasdone using SPSS advanced statistical software version 22.0(SPSS Inc., Chicago, IL, USA).a 2017 The International Society of DermatologyInternational Journal of Dermatology 2017ResultsThe initial search and manual screening of the referencesyielded 370 citations. Of the 362 unique articles, 102 wereretained as candidate studies based on their titles and abstracts.

Followingfull-textreview,40articleswereincluded.Theincluded studies were then compiled according to treatmentmodality. All the studies were published between 1969 and2016. Twenty-six studies used surgery (Table 1), five used scle-rotherapy, four used cryotherapy, two used corticosteroid injec-tion, five used manual compression of the cyst content, twoused CO2 laser vaporization, and two used infrared coagulation(Table 2). The stepwise approach for study selection is summa-rized in Fig. 1. Studies were further divided into nine subgroupsdepending on the excised component (cyst, overlying skin, andosteophyte) and the wound coverage techniques (primary clo-sure, skin graft, or local skin flap). All studies comprise 1364DMC lesions. The mean age of patients was 60.47 years, with arange of 13-89 and 31.79% of patients were males. Most com-monly reported pre-treatment symptoms were pain, osteoarthri-tis, and nail deformities. Mean time of the duration of symptomsuntil patients sought treatment was 13 months. Ninety-four per-cent of lesions were located on the fingers, and 6% on the toes;61% were on the right side and 39% on the left side. The DMC

were located on the third finger in 38%, on the second in 26%,on the thumb in 18%, on the fourth finger in 9%, and on the fifthin 8% (Fig. 2). The most commonly reported adverse events foreach treatment modality are reported in Table 3.The total cure rate for all treatments, combined, was 83%.The cure rate for surgical management was 95%. Within thesurgical methods, cyst and osteophyte excision with primaryclosure had the highest cure rate (98%) with the largest numberof treated lesions (218) and was chosen as a reference forcomparison with the remaining surgical techniques. Simple cystexcision, destruction of cyst pedicle and skin, and cyst andosteophyte excision with skin grafting showed a statistically sig-nificant difference when compared to the reference subgroup.The five most frequently used treatments for DMC were surgery(n = 849), followed by expression of cyst content (n = 132),sclerotherapy (n = 119), corticosteroid injection (n = 108), andcryotherapy (n = 103). Surgery yielded the highest cure rateamong all treatment modalities (95%) with a statistically signifi-

cantdifferencewhencomparedtosclerotherapy(77%),cryotherapy (72%), corticosteroid injection (61%), and expres-sion of cyst content (39%) (P < 0.001). Within the nonsurgicaltherapy subgroups, sclerotherapy had the highest cure rate(77%) and was superior to corticosteroid injection (P = 0.008)and expression of cyst content (P < 0.001) but not to cryother-apy (P = 0.350). Corticosteroid injection was superior to expres-sion of cyst content (P = 0.001). Results are summarized inDiscussionTo date, no treatment showed a consistent success as DMC is ahighly recurrent lesion. In the included studies, several surgicaltechniques have been described for the treatment of DMC. Theyconsisted of cyst excision with or without concomitant debride-ment of underlying joint osteophytes. Overlying skin, even whenSurgeryJoint stiffnessNail deformityBleedingInfectionPain and tendernessTendon injurySclerotherapyPainSuperficial necrosisJoint stiffnessInflammation and erythemaCryotherapyPainDiscomfortHemorrhagic blisterCorticosteroid injectionInfectionStiffnessExpression of cyst contentInflammationCO2 laser vaporizationScarringInfrared coagulationPainErythemaBlisteringScarringInfectionInternational Journal of Dermatology 2017a 2017 The International Society of Dermatologythinned, could heal and provide good coverage.8,10,19,24-34 How-ever, it was frequently excised along with the cyst. In this case,the defect was closed either by a full thickness skin graft25,34,35 or

by a local flap.10,20,22,36-40 In our review, we demonstrated thatsurgery had the highest cure rate among the five most commonlyused treatments: all surgical techniques had a cure rate superiorto 90% with a mean of 95%. Comparisons are being made: surgery versus sclerotherapy, surgery versus cryotherapy, surgery versus corticos-teroid injection, surgery versus expression of cyst content, sclerotherapy versus cryotherapy, sclerotherapy versus corticosteroid injection,sclerotherapy versus expression of cyst content, and corticosteroid injection versus expression of cyst content.a 2017 The International Society of DermatologyInternational Journal of Dermatology 2017pain and tenderness, and tendon injury), contraindications to sur-gery, and relatively high cost.Five studies evaluated sclerotherapy as a treatment modalityfor DMC. Cure rate was 77%, which was statistically inferior tothat of surgery (P < 0.001). The injected sclerotic agents were:polidocanol,41,42sodiumtetradecylsulfate,43,44andOK432(lyophilized strep pyogenes).45 In four of the five studies, treat-ment had to be repeated at fixed intervals to ensure cystremoval.41-44 Cryotherapy is another noninvasive treatmentmethod for DMC. Liquid nitrogen was applied either with aprobe or by the means of an open spray technique.46-49 Thetotal cure rate was 72%, which was lower than the surgery curerate (P < 0.001) but was not statistically different from that ofsclerotherapy (P = 0.350).Both sclerotherapy and cryotherapy are easy, not expensive,

and noninvasive treatment modalities that offer good alterna-tives for nonsurgical candidates as they yield acceptable curerates with a good safety profile.Two studies used corticosteroid injections to treat the cystswith a total cure rate of 61%.33,50 Simple expression of cystcontent was done by needling26,51 or needle aspiration52,53 andhad a total cure rate of 39%. CO2 laser vaporization and infra-red coagulation were only used in two small case serieseach12,17,18,54 with a total cure rate of 88 and 76%, respectively.

Surgery should be considered as the first-line treatment forDMC as it is the most frequently used modality with the highestcure rate. Special attention should be made to the possibleMean age of patients with DMC is 60.47 years, and 31.79% of patientsare malesSixty-one percent of DMC occur on the right hand and 39% on the leftIn the hand, the DMC are located on the thumb in 18%, on the secondfinger in 26%, on the third in 38%, on the fourth in 9%, and on the fifthin 8%The five most frequently used treatments for DMC are surgery, followedby expression of cyst content, sclerotherapy, corticosteroid injection,and cryotherapySurgery has the highest cure rate among all treatment modalities (95%)followed by sclerotherapy (77%), cryotherapy (72%), corticosteroidinjection (61%), and expression of cyst content (39%)Surgery targets are the cyst, the overlying skin, and the underlyingosteophytesThe wound coverage techniques are: primary closure, skin graft, andlocal skin flapThe overlying skin, even when thinned, can heal and provide goodcoverageFor surgical management, attention should be made to the possibleadverse events, and these must be clearly explained to the patientSclerotherapy and cryotherapy may require multiple treatment sessionsbut are less invasive than surgery and can be good alternatives fornonsurgical candidatesCorticosteroid injection and expression of cyst content are frequentlyused treatment modalities but have lower cure rates compared tosurgery, sclerotherapy, and cryotherapyOther less frequently used modalities include CO2 laser vaporization andinfrared coagulationDMC, digital mucous cyst.International Journal of Dermatology 2017a 2017 The International Society of Dermatologyadverse events that must be clearly explained to the patientprior to treatment choice. Second-line treatments include scle-rotherapy and cryotherapy. They may have less adverse eventsbut can require multiple sessions. This could influence both the

patient's and physician's choice of therapy. Third-line treatmentsinclude less efficient or less studied methods such as corticos-teroid injection, expression of cyst content, CO2 laser vaporiza-tion, and infrared coagulation (Fig. 3).There are several limitations to this review. Thirty-four of theincluded studies were retrospective and six were prospective tri-als. We did not find any randomized controlled trials comparingdifferent treatment methods, which prevented us from perform-ing a meta-analysis. Other limitations were inter-practitioner dis-parities and publication bias as only published articles wereevaluated.ConclusionThis review provides a practical treatment algorithm for DMCbased on cure rates and guides the physician in this much-debated field. Based on available data, we found that the mostcommonly used treatment methods are surgery, sclerotherapy,cryotherapy, corticosteroid injection, and expression of cyst con-tent. Among these modalities, surgery yielded the highest curerates. Future adequately designed randomized controlled trialsare warranted to compare the efficacy of these techniques.Questions (answers provided afterreferences)(1) What are the most common age group and gender ofpatients presenting with digital myxoid cysts?a. Young males.b. Young females.c. Old males.d. Old females.(2) Which is the most affected finger?a. First.b. Second.c. Third.d. Fourth.e. Fifth.(3) What are the main reported symptoms?a. Pain.b.
